lesson.menu_title: Verbs That Take 'To' and Verbs That Take '-ing' -- Making Plans
lesson.menu_desc: Make plans and say what you prefer with the two verb patterns: want to, need to, decide to, hope to, arrange to (verb + TO) and enjoy, avoid, keep, postpone, mind (verb + -ING) -- with the false-friend trap 'I am looking forward to meeting you' and the survival phrase 'Would you mind...?'. A reading about two coordinators who prepare for Seoul in two different ways. Vocabulary: improve, hope, arrange, tend, prefer, keep, postpone, mind. Expression: 'I am looking forward to...' Idiom: 'Keep it up.' -- 33 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 14 -- Verbs That Take 'To' and Verbs That Take '-ing'
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Maria Clara | Lesson 14 -- Verbs That Take 'To' and Verbs That Take '-ing'

listenings.text: Hi Maria Clara, it is Bruno. Just a quick note before we plan the Seoul trip together. I want to confirm a few things. First, I arranged to meet the Korean team on Monday, so we need to prepare the documents this week. I know you prefer to travel light, so I suggest one small bag. Also, the rector hopes to join the first dinner, but he tends to arrive late, so please do not worry. And one more thing: I decided to postpone the budget call to Friday. I really enjoy working with you on these plans. Keep it up, and call me if you want to change anything. Thank you.

For twenty years, I used to bring an interpreter to every meeting. I wanted to speak, but I preferred to stay silent. I avoided English, because I was afraid to make mistakes. Then something changed. I decided to improve, one day at a time. Now I keep practicing every morning, even when I am tired. I enjoy meeting people from other countries, and I am not afraid anymore. I still make mistakes, but I do not mind. In November, I want to lead the delegation in Seoul myself. I am looking forward to speaking for my own institution. And one day, I hope to teach my granddaughter, Clarinha, to be brave too.

paras: A director had to choose one coordinator to lead a delegation to Seoul. She called two people to her office. "How do you want to prepare?" she asked each one.

Paulo answered first. He tends to avoid English, and he prefers to bring an interpreter. "I hope to improve one day," he said, "but this year I want to postpone it. I do not enjoy speaking in front of people."

Then Renata answered. She arranged to meet a teacher every week, and she keeps practicing at home. "I do not mind making mistakes," she said. "I mind staying silent. I want to lead the meeting in English myself." Six months later, at the airport in Seoul, one coordinator spoke for the whole delegation. It was Renata. She was not afraid, because she was ready.
The director learned one thing that day. The best English is not the perfect English. It is the English of the person who keeps trying.
reading.source: Institutional & Travel English -- narrative written for Maria Clara's package

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of this story?
choices: Paulo and Renata travelled to Seoul together to lead the delegation.
The coordinator who kept practicing and was ready to speak got to lead -- the one who avoided English did not.
A delegation should always travel with a professional interpreter.

items: The director had to choose one coordinator to lead the delegation.
t
"A director had to choose one coordinator to lead a delegation to Seoul."
Paulo enjoys speaking in front of people.
f
"I do not enjoy speaking in front of people."
Renata arranged to meet a teacher every week.
t
"She arranged to meet a teacher every week, and she keeps practicing at home."
Renata prefers to stay silent in meetings.
f
"I do not mind making mistakes. I mind staying silent."
Renata spoke for the whole delegation in Seoul.
t
"one coordinator spoke for the whole delegation. It was Renata."

bank.label: Making plans and expressing preferences: the two patterns
items: I want to improve my English before November.
I decided to lead the meeting myself.
I enjoy meeting people from other countries.
I keep practicing every day.
Would you mind repeating that, please?
I am looking forward to meeting the rector.

items.situation: A colleague offers you an interpreter for the Seoul meeting. Say, politely, that you prefer to speak English yourself.
tips: Thank you, but I prefer to speak English myself.

items.situation: A waiter in Seoul speaks very fast. Ask him, politely, to say it again.
tips: Would you mind repeating that, please?

items.situation: The rector asks about your goal for this year. Tell him one thing you want to get better at.
tips: I want to improve my English before November.

items.situation: Your assistant asks what you do every day for your English. Answer with your routine.
A meeting is on a bad day for you. Ask to move it to Friday.
tips: Can we postpone the meeting to Friday?

items.situation: Tell a new colleague one thing you enjoy and one thing you avoid about English.
tips: I enjoy meeting new people, but I avoid speaking in large groups.

pressure.title: Answer live
items: In one sentence: what do you want to improve this year, and how do you keep practicing?
You are in a shop in Seoul. Ask the assistant three things politely with "Would you mind...?". Do not stop.
Tell me your English journey in thirty seconds: what you used to do, what you enjoy now, and what you are looking forward to.

pressure.text: want / need / decide / hope / plan / arrange / tend / prefer + TO + verb. enjoy / avoid / keep / postpone / mind + verb-ING. And after 'looking forward to', use -ing -- here 'to' is a preposition, not an infinitive.
